A language even simpler than me? The computer world only has 0 and 1!
A language even simpler than me? The computer world only has 0 and 1

Basically whenever it was computer class, I'd use the "force-close" trick to escape the teacher's control. But one time, for whatever reason, I felt like actually listening for once (yes, just that random).
"In the computer world, everything you see is composed of 0s and 1s." the teacher wrote on the board.
"Huh? What? Only 0 and 1? Then where do colors come from?" my brain wondered.
"Curious about how colors are made? They're also composed of 0s and 1s." the teacher continued, like a mind-reader.
"You remember the RGB triplet, right? R = Red, G = Green, B = Blue — combining these three produces all the colors." She drew three circles labeled R, G, B on the board.
"Even the A, B, C letters on the screen are composed of 0s and 1s. That uses something called ASCII." She wrote "ASCII" big on the board.
"WOW! So computers can't directly understand A, B, C!" I thought it was pretty cool.
…But then I tuned out and went back to gaming as usual.
"Hey, XXX, are you done with Warcraft yet?" I asked the classmate next to me while using the "force-close" trick to escape the teacher's control.
"Almost done. Are you playing the next round?" he answered, focused on his characters.
"Yeah, wanna use GGC to play against randoms?" I asked.
GGC is a social gaming platform. Since Warcraft only supports LAN play, players use GGC to simulate LAN and play against others.
You can guess the consequence of not listening — computer-class tests and homework were entirely beyond me.
What's binary? Binary is a number system using only two symbols — 0 and 1.
"Only computers use binary?" you might wonder.
No — anything tied to electronic circuits uses binary. Why binary? Couldn't we use octal, decimal, hex, etc.?
Binary circuit design is relatively simple — only two states (on = 1, off = 0), making circuits easier to design and implement.
Binary has higher fault tolerance for electronic signals.
Some terms here might be unfamiliar, so let me explain "
Fault tolerance is the system's tolerance range when encountering errors or failures — the ability to keep running even when some parts fail. Plain language: "
something went wrong, but the system keeps running
Electronic signals are voltage changes in a circuit, used to represent numbers. E.g., 0V ~ 0.8V = 0, 2V ~ 5V = 1, and 0.8V ~ 2V represents uncertain state — also known as "
". Electronic signals are voltage changes, while data is the meaning carried by those changes. In other words, electronic signals are the "carrier" of data, not data itself.
Example: to send the number 5, its binary is 101. Converted to electronic signals, it might be "5V, 0V, 5V" — the voltage pattern represents 5, and the computer decodes it back to 101.
Before we get into binary, let's quickly recap "
" — the system we use every day.
Decimal uses 10 symbols (0–9). After counting 0 to 9, the next number "10" works as: units reset to 0, the previous digit increments by 1. That's "
What about binary? Only 0 and 1. When you hit "2", since it's out of range, units reset to 0, the previous digit increments by 1. So from "1" you go to "10".
Quick exercise — convert decimal to binary.
Don't worry, I'll walk you through.
repeatedly divide by 2, record the remainders
"Quotient" is the result of division. "Remainder" is what's left over.
" — only keeping the integer part and discarding the fractional part. The discarded fraction becomes the "remainder".
